growth_records.cite: Hara, Takeshi et al. Interspecies metabolite transfer fuels the methionine metabolism of Fusobacterium nucleatum to stimulate volatile methyl mercaptan production. mSystems (2024). DOI:10.1128/msystems.00764-23. [PMC10878106]
growth_records.snippet: Fusobacterium nucleatum subsp. nucleatum ATCC25586 ... L-Methionine 477.71 ± 26.30 (ppm CH3SH). F. nucleatum gradually consumed methionine, about 50% in the substrate present during 12 h of incubation ... Glutamate present in the mCDM was f
growth_records.notes: Resting washed-cell suspension assay (OD600=1.0), NOT a growth culture; OD 'virtually unchanged' after 16 h (Table S2) so no growth_rate/doubling_time derivable (mu~0). CH3SH secretion 477.71+/-26.30 ppm is a headspace GC value at 5 mM L-methionine, pH 6.5, 16 h (Table 1); a comparable ~500 ppm plateau at 3.5-5.0 mM Met at pH 6.5 is shown in Fig 2A. Methionine uptake reported only as ~50% of the supplied substrate over 12 h (Fig 3); the exact starting methionine concentration for the Fig 3 time-course is not explicitly stated (mCDM was supplemented with 0.5-5.0 mM Met 'as necessary'), so an absolute mmol or mmol/gDW/h uptake rate cannot be computed without assumption; no gDW-per-OD conversion is given, precluding biomass-specific normalization. 13C5,15N-methionine tracing (10 mM, 6 h) was performed but reported as relative isotopomer abundances (m+n) over time, not net fluxes with units, so mfa_fluxes left empty.

growth_records.strain: F. nucleatum ATCC 25586 + S. gordonii DL1 WT

up.units: mM net over 12 h (inferred: 1.2 mM S. gordonii mono - 0.95 mM coculture)
up.method: UPLC; difference of ornithine released in S. gordonii monoculture vs coculture

sec.method: gas chromatography with flame photometric detector

growth_records.cite: Hara, Takeshi et al. Interspecies metabolite transfer fuels the methionine metabolism of Fusobacterium nucleatum to stimulate volatile methyl mercaptan production. mSystems (2024). DOI:10.1128/msystems.00764-23. [PMC10878106]
growth_records.snippet: cocultures of F. nucleatum and S. gordonii WT exhibited the highest level of consumption of methionine ... release of 1.2 and 0.95 mM ornithine, respectively ... suggesting an uptake of 0.25 mM ornithine by F. nucleatum ... approximately 2.
growth_records.notes: Coculture of two resting washed-cell suspensions (each OD600=1.0); no net growth reported (bacterial growth not significantly affected by ornithine addition, data not shown; OD unchanged). Net ornithine accumulation in coculture = 0.95 mM over 12 h -> 0.95/12 = 0.079 mM/h. Inferred F. nucleatum ornithine uptake = 1.2 mM (S. gordonii monoculture) - 0.95 mM (coculture) = 0.25 mM over 12 h, as stated verbatim by authors. CH3SH enhancement ~2.3-fold (0.5 mM Met) to ~3-fold (1 mM Met) vs monocultures; enhancement preserved in non-contact coculture (diffusible factor = ornithine). 13C5,15N-methionine intracellular tracing (CE-TOF-MS, 6 h) mapped fate of Met into methionine cycle and polyamine/salvage pathway but reported only relative isotopomer abundances (m+n), not net fluxes with units -> mfa_fluxes empty. Gene-expression: mgl/metK up 2.3-4.2-fold, metK/metH/metQ/metI up 4.0-5.6-fold in coculture (qPCR), consistent with enhanced Met uptake.

growth_records.medium: Chemically defined medium (van der Hoeven), glucose+amino-acid supplemented

up.method: HPLC / medium balance; expressed per g cell protein (not per hour)
derived: qGlc = D x (glucose per g protein) = 0.069/h x 23.1 = 1.594

sec.method: derived: q = D x value = 0.069 x 92.4 = 6.376

product_yields.note: Molar product/glucose ratios (mmol product per mmol glucose consumed) at pH 7.4: formate 92.4/23.1=4.00; acetate 59.4/23.1=2.57; butanoate 63.0/23.1=2.73; lactate 5.7/23.1=0.247. NB much of the acetate/butanoate/formate also derives from amino-acid (glutamate/histidine/lysine) catabolism, not glucose alone, so these ratios overstate glucose-specific yield.

growth_records.cite: Chew, Jactty et al. A proteomic investigation of Fusobacterium nucleatum alkaline-induced biofilms. BMC Microbiology (2012). DOI:10.1186/1471-2180-12-189. [PMC3478200]
growth_records.snippet: medium ... supplemented with 10 mM glucose, 20 mM glutamic acid, 10 mM histidine and 10 mM lysine (all other amino acids were 1 mM) ... D=0.069/h ... generation time 10 h. Table 2 pH 7.4: glucose 23.1+/-2.1; lactate 5.7; formate 92.4; aceta
growth_records.notes: Chemostat steady-state, anaerobic, 37C, pH 7.4 planktonic. mu=D=0.069/h at steady state; doubling time = ln2/D = 0.6931/0.069 = 10.05 h (matches stated generation time ~10 h). Table 2 values are expressed as mmol per g cell protein (uptake footnote 1; acidic end-products footnote 3 in mmol g-1 cell protein), i.e. amount per biomass, NOT per hour. Derived specific rates use qi = D x (amount per g protein): glucose 0.069x23.1=1.594; formate 0.069x92.4=6.376; acetate 0.069x59.4=4.099; butanoate 0.069x63.0=4.347; lactate 0.069x5.7=0.393 (all mmol/g cell protein/h). GDH activity 8.87+/-0.40 unit/mg protein; intracellular polyglucose 2.39+/-0.12 ug glucose/mg protein. Amino acids glutamate/histidine/lysine are co-catabolised to acetate+butanoate+ammonia, so acid end-products are not solely from glucose.

growth_records.medium: Chemically defined medium (van der Hoeven), glucose+amino-acid supplemented

up.method: HPLC / medium balance; expressed per g cell protein (not per hour)

product_yields.note: Molar product/glucose ratios at pH 8.2: formate 131.2/65.9=1.99; acetate 115.3/65.9=1.75; butanoate 99.6/65.9=1.51; lactate 18.3/65.9=0.278. Acid end-products also arise from amino-acid catabolism, so ratios overstate glucose-specific yield. Note ~3-fold rise in glucose utilisation and shift toward lactate at pH 8.2.

growth_records.cite: Chew, Jactty et al. A proteomic investigation of Fusobacterium nucleatum alkaline-induced biofilms. BMC Microbiology (2012). DOI:10.1186/1471-2180-12-189. [PMC3478200]
growth_records.snippet: Table 2 pH 8.2: glucose utilisation 65.9+/-7.2; IP 7.62; lactate 18.3+/-1.9; formate 131.2+/-11.6; acetate 115.3+/-12.7; butanoate 99.6+/-10.8 (mmol/g cell protein); GDH 13.73+/-1.25 unit/mg. Growth pH raised to 8.2 induced biofilm.
growth_records.notes: Biofilm (attached) cells at pH 8.2, anaerobic, 37C, same chemostat vessel with feed D=0.069/h. Specific growth rate of biofilm cells is NOT equal to D (biofilm is retained, not washed out), so no per-hour qS derived here (undefined biomass-specific growth rate for the adherent population); values reported only as mmol per g cell protein (Table 2 footnotes 1 and 3). Relative to pH 7.4, glucose utilisation rose ~2.85x (65.9 vs 23.1), lactate 3.2x (18.3 vs 5.7), formate 1.42x, acetate 1.94x, butanoate 1.58x, intracellular polyglucose 3.2x, GDH activity 1.55x. Acetate/butanoate/formate partly from glutamate/histidine/lysine catabolism.
